title_en: Compliant and Contrary Flow in the Fat and the Thin
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Qibo: "I have received instruction from you, Master, in the way of the needle, and its many aspects have all been made clear to me. Yet the way you apply it seems as effortless as if something were simply released, and there appears to be nothing fixed or rigid about it. Is this mastery something you gained through long study and practice, or does it arise in your mind through careful observation of things?" Qibo said: "Sages, in practicing the Way, accord above with Heaven, accord below with Earth, and accord in the middle with human affairs. There must always be a clear method by which measures and numbers are established, and models and standards by which things are checked and verified, before the teaching can be transmitted to others. Thus a craftsman cannot set aside the foot-rule and simply guess at length, nor cast off the ink-line and expect to strike a level. Nor can an artisan dispense with the compass and still draw a circle, or discard the square and still form a right angle. To know how to apply these instruments is simply to make use of the natural properties of things — it is a teaching easily put into practice, the constant principle of compliant and contrary flow."

Huangdi said: "I wish to hear what is meant by according with the natural properties of things." Qibo said: "If one stands over a deep channel and breaches it, the water will drain away of itself without the need for great effort. If one follows an already-cut, obstructed watercourse and breaches its blockage, the channel can be made to flow through freely. This is a way of speaking about the smoothness or roughness of qi, and the clarity or turbidity of blood and fluids, and about the compliant or contrary directions in which they travel."

Huangdi said: "I wish to hear whether people who are fair or dark, fat or thin, short or tall, each have their own proper measure for needling." Qibo said: "When a person is in the prime of life, robust and large, with blood and qi abundant and full, and skin and flesh firm and solid, if evil qi then invades, one should needle deeply and retain the needle — such is the fat person. Consider one with broad shoulders and neck, thin flesh but thick skin, and a dark complexion, with heavy, drooping lips — such a person's blood is dark and turbid, and the qi is rough and slow-moving. In temperament, such a person tends to be greedy in taking and giving. In needling this type, one should needle deeply, retain the needle, and considerably increase the count."

Huangdi said: "How does one needle a thin person?" Qibo said: "The thin person has thin skin and a pale complexion, with lean, spare flesh, thin lips, and a light manner of speech. Such a person's blood is clear and the qi flows smoothly; qi is easily lost and blood is easily damaged. In needling this type, one should needle shallowly and withdraw the needle quickly."

Huangdi said: "How does one needle an ordinary person?" Qibo said: "One observes whether the complexion is fair or dark and regulates accordingly for each. In one who is upright and of generous, substantial build, the blood and qi are harmonious and well regulated; in needling this type, one need only follow the ordinary measure without deviation."

Huangdi said: "How does one needle a robust man of sturdy bone?" Qibo said: "In needling a robust man of sturdy bone, with firm flesh and supple joints, of imposing bearing — if such a person is of a heavy, ponderous constitution, the qi will be rough and the blood turbid; in needling this type, one should needle deeply and retain the needle, considerably increasing the count. If such a person is of a vigorous, forceful constitution, the qi will flow smoothly and the blood will be clear; in needling this type, one should needle shallowly and withdraw the needle quickly."

Huangdi said: "How does one needle an infant?" Qibo said: "In the infant, the flesh is tender and delicate, the blood scant and the qi weak. In needling this type, one should use the fine hair-needle, insert it shallowly, and withdraw it quickly; this may be done twice a day."

Huangdi said: "What is meant by standing over a deep channel and breaching it?" Qibo said: "When the blood is clear and the qi turbid, if one drains it rapidly, the qi will become exhausted." Huangdi said: "What is meant by following an obstructed watercourse and breaching its blockage?" Qibo said: "When the blood is turbid and the qi is rough, if one drains it rapidly, the channel can be made to flow through freely."

Huangdi said: "What is the compliant and contrary course along which the vessels travel?" Qibo said: "The three yin channels of the hand travel from the viscera to the hand; the three yang channels of the hand travel from the hand to the head; the three yang channels of the foot travel from the head to the foot; and the three yin channels of the foot travel from the foot to the abdomen."

Huangdi said: "Why is it that the Shaoyin vessel alone travels downward?" Qibo said: "It is not so. The Penetrating Vessel is the sea of the five viscera and six bowels, and the five viscera and six bowels all draw upon it. Its upper branch emerges at the pharyngeal cavity behind the nose, where it seeps into all the yang channels and irrigates all the essence. Its lower branch pours into the great network vessel of the Shaoyin, emerges at Qichong (the Qi Thoroughfare), travels along the inner border of the yin aspect of the thigh, enters the popliteal fossa, runs hidden along the inside of the shin bone, and descends to a point behind the inner ankle, where it attaches and then divides. One descending branch joins with the Shaoyin channel and seeps into the three yin channels; another branch running in front travels hidden and emerges to attach at the instep, descends along the instep, and enters between the great toe, seeping into all the network vessels and warming the flesh and muscles. Hence, when the branch network vessel becomes bound and obstructed, the instep will not move; when it does not move, this is reversal, and when there is reversal, there will be cold." Huangdi said: "How can this be verified?" Qibo said: "One guides the patient with words, palpates and tests accordingly; if the pulsation at that point is not present as it should be, then the compliant and contrary course of the flow may still be discerned." Huangdi said: "How profound indeed is the sages' practice of the Way! Bright as the sun and moon, discerning as fine as the tip of a hair — were it not for you, Master, who could expound it?"

title_en: Discourse on the Blood Network Vessels
paragraphs_en: Huangdi said: "I wish to hear about the strange evils that do not reside within the channels." Qibo said: "These are found in the blood network vessels."

Huangdi said: "When one needles a blood network vessel and the patient collapses, what does this mean? When the blood spurts out in a jet, what does this mean? When the blood is scant, dark, and turbid, what does this mean? When the blood that flows out is clear and half of it is like a watery fluid, what does this mean? When swelling occurs after the needle is withdrawn, what does this mean? When the blood that flows out is sometimes copious, sometimes scant, and the complexion turns pale and ashen, what does this mean? When the complexion does not change after the needle is withdrawn but the patient feels vexed and oppressed, what does this mean? When much blood is let yet the patient shows no distress at all, what does this mean? I wish to hear the reasons for these."
Qibo said: "When the vessel qi is exuberant but the blood is deficient, needling will cause the qi to collapse and be lost; when qi collapses, the patient falls."

When blood and qi are both exuberant and yin qi predominates, the blood flows smoothly, and needling causes it to spurt out. When yang qi has accumulated and stagnated for a long time without being drained, the blood is dark and turbid, and therefore cannot spurt out.

When one has just drunk fluids and they have seeped into the network vessels without yet blending with the blood, the blood that flows out will be accompanied by a separate watery fluid. In one who has not recently drunk fluids, if there is water lodged within the body, in time this will become swelling.
When yin qi has accumulated within yang and this qi has settled into the network vessels, then upon needling, before the blood emerges the qi moves out first, and so swelling results.

When the qi of yin and yang have only just come together and have not yet fully harmonized, if one then drains them, both yin and yang collapse together, exterior and interior become separated from each other, and so the complexion is drained of color and turns pale and ashen.

When needling causes much blood to flow out, yet the complexion does not change though the patient feels vexed and oppressed, this is because the network vessel has been needled while the channel itself has been left deficient; when the deficient channel belongs to yin, yin then collapses, and so the patient feels vexed and oppressed.

When yin and yang come together and combine to form impediment (bi), this is because pathogenic qi overflows the channels within and pours into the network vessels without. In such a case, both yin and yang have a surplus, and so even though much blood is let out, this cannot cause deficiency.

Huangdi said: "How does one assess this?" Qibo said: "The blood vessels to be needled are exuberant, firm, distended, and red, running crosswise without a fixed location above or below; the smaller ones are like a needle, the larger ones like a sinew. To needle and drain them according to this observation is entirely safe, and one will not fail to hit the proper measure. If one fails to observe the proper measure and acts contrary to it, the consequences will each correspond to their own degree of error."

Huangdi said: "Why does the flesh sometimes cling to the needle once it has been inserted?" Qibo said: "When heat qi gathers around the needle, the needle becomes hot; once hot, the flesh clings to the needle internally, and so it becomes firmly fixed."

title_en: Yin and Yang, the Clear and the Turbid
paragraphs_en: Huangdi said: "I have heard that the twelve channel vessels correspond to the twelve channel-waters of the land, each of which differs in its five colors and its clarity or turbidity. Yet in a person the blood and qi appear to be uniform — how, then, do they correspond to this diversity?" Qibo said: "If the blood and qi of a person were truly uniform throughout, then all under Heaven would be as one — how, then, could there be any disorder?" Huangdi said: "I am asking about a single person, not about the multitudes of all under Heaven." Qibo said: "Even within a single person there is disordered qi, just as within the pattern of all under Heaven there are disordered people — they correspond to one and the same principle."

Huangdi said: "I wish to hear about the clear and turbid aspects of human qi." Qibo said: "That which receives grain is turbid; that which receives breath-qi is clear. The clear pours into yin, and the turbid pours into yang. Of the turbid, that portion which is clear rises to emerge at the throat; of the clear, that portion which is turbid descends and flows downward. When clear and turbid interfere with one another, this is called disordered qi."

Huangdi said: "Now, yin is clear and yang is turbid, yet within the turbid there is a clear portion, and within the clear there is a turbid portion. How does one distinguish the clear from the turbid?" Qibo said: "In the great division of qi, the clear rises to pour into the lung, and the turbid descends to flow into the stomach. The clear qi of the stomach rises to emerge at the mouth; the turbid qi of the lung descends to pour into the channels and accumulates internally in the sea of qi."

Huangdi said: "All the yang channels are turbid — which yang channel is most turbid?" Qibo said: "The Hand Taiyang Small Intestine channel alone receives the turbid portion of yang, while the Hand Taiyin Lung channel alone receives the clear portion of yin. The clear portion rises to travel to the body's open orifices, while the turbid portion descends to travel through the various channels. All the yin channels are clear, yet the Foot Taiyin Spleen channel alone receives a turbid portion."

Huangdi said: "How does one treat this?" Qibo said: "The qi of the clear is smooth-flowing, while the qi of the turbid is rough — this is the constant nature of qi. Thus, in needling yin, one needles deeply and retains the needle; in needling yang, one needles shallowly and withdraws quickly. Where clear and turbid interfere with one another, one regulates according to their proper measure."

title_en: Yin and Yang Correlated with the Sun and Moon
paragraphs_en: Huangdi said: "I have heard that Heaven is yang and Earth is yin, that the sun is yang and the moon is yin. How do these correspond to the human body?" Qibo said: "Above the waist corresponds to Heaven, and below the waist corresponds to Earth; thus Heaven is yang and Earth is yin. Accordingly, the twelve channel vessels of the feet correspond to the twelve months. The moon is generated from water, and so that which lies below is yin. The ten fingers of the hands correspond to the ten suns — the ten heavenly stems used to mark the days; the sun governs fire, and so that which lies above is yang."

Huangdi said: "How does this correspond to the vessels?" Qibo said: "Yin, the third earthly branch, is the first month, when yang is generated; it governs the Shaoyang of the left foot. Wei, the eighth branch, is the sixth month; it governs the Shaoyang of the right foot. Mao, the fourth branch, is the second month; it governs the Taiyang of the left foot. Wu, the seventh branch, is the fifth month; it governs the Taiyang of the right foot. Chen, the fifth branch, is the third month; it governs the Yangming of the left foot. Si, the sixth branch, is the fourth month; it governs the Yangming of the right foot. These two yang channels come together at the front, and so this is called Yangming, the confluence of yang. Shen, the ninth branch, is the seventh month, when yin is generated; it governs the Shaoyin of the right foot. Chou, the second branch, is the twelfth month; it governs the Shaoyin of the left foot. You, the tenth branch, is the eighth month; it governs the Taiyin of the right foot. Zi, the first branch, is the eleventh month; it governs the Taiyin of the left foot. Xu, the eleventh branch, is the ninth month; it governs the Jueyin of the right foot. Hai, the twelfth branch, is the tenth month; it governs the Jueyin of the left foot. These two yin channels meet and are exhausted together, and so this is called Jueyin."

Jia, the first heavenly stem, governs the Shaoyang of the left hand; Ji, the sixth stem, governs the Shaoyang of the right hand. Yi, the second stem, governs the Taiyang of the left hand; Wu, the fifth stem, governs the Taiyang of the right hand. Bing, the third stem, governs the Yangming of the left hand; Ding, the fourth stem, governs the Yangming of the right hand — these two fires unite together, and so this too is called Yangming. Geng, the seventh stem, governs the Shaoyin of the right hand; Gui, the tenth stem, governs the Shaoyin of the left hand; Xin, the eighth stem, governs the Taiyin of the right hand; Ren, the ninth stem, governs the Taiyin of the left hand.

Thus the yang channels of the foot are the lesser yang within yin; the yin channels of the foot are the greater yin within yin. The yang channels of the hand are the greater yang within yang; the yin channels of the hand are the lesser yin within yang. That which is above the waist is yang; that which is below the waist is yin.

With regard to the five viscera: the heart is the greater yang within yang; the lung is the lesser yin within yin; the liver is the lesser yang within yin; the spleen is the utmost yin within yin; and the kidney is the greater yin within yin.

Huangdi said: "How does one apply this in treatment?" Qibo said: "In the first, second, and third months, human qi resides on the left, so one should not needle the yang channels of the left foot. In the fourth, fifth, and sixth months, human qi resides on the right, so one should not needle the yang channels of the right foot. In the seventh, eighth, and ninth months, human qi resides on the right, so one should not needle the yin channels of the right foot. In the tenth, eleventh, and twelfth months, human qi resides on the left, so one should not needle the yin channels of the left foot."

Huangdi said: "Among the five phases, the east corresponds to Jia and Yi, which belong to wood and govern spring. Spring's color is green-blue, and it governs the liver; the liver corresponds to the Foot Jueyin. Yet now you say that Jia governs the Shaoyang of the left hand — this does not accord with the numerical sequence. Why is this?" Qibo said: "This concerns the yin and yang of Heaven and Earth, not the sequential progression of the four seasons and five phases. Moreover, yin and yang have names but no fixed form; hence they may be counted as ten, divided into a hundred, dispersed into a thousand, and extended into ten thousand — this is precisely what is meant."

Huangdi said: "I have received instruction in the nine needles from you, Master, and have also privately studied various other methods of treatment — daoyin exercises to guide the qi, qiaomo massage, moxibustion, hot compresses, puncture, cauterization, and the drinking of medicinal decoctions. Should one hold to a single one of these exclusively, or ought one to practice all of them together?" Qibo said: "These various methods are methods suited to different people; no single person can practice all of them at once."

Huangdi said: "This, then, is what is meant by holding to the one without losing it, whereby the myriad things are all accomplished. I have already heard of the essentials of yin and yang, the principles of deficiency and excess, the errors that come from their tipping and shifting out of balance, and the categories of what can be treated. I now wish to hear of the transformations of disease — of how it spreads and overwhelms until it becomes fatal and untreatable. May I hear of this?" Qibo said: "How essential a question this is! Bright, it is like waking from sleep into daylight; obscure, it is like being lost in the darkness of night. If one can take this in and put it into practice, the spirit will develop together with it; once one has fully absorbed it, the spirit will naturally attain it of itself. The principle by which the spirit is generated can be written down on bamboo and silk, but it cannot be truly handed down to one's own descendants without their own realization of it."

Huangdi said: "What is meant by 'waking into daylight'?" Qibo said: "To be clear about yin and yang is like having one's confusion resolved, like waking from drunkenness." Huangdi said: "What is meant by 'the darkness of night'?" Qibo said: "It is mute, without sound; vast and indistinct, without form. The hair and skin's texture are damaged, the upright qi is thrown into disorder, the invasive evil spreads and overflows, and is transmitted through the blood vessels. When the great evil qi enters the viscera, causing abdominal pain that spreads downward, this can bring about death and cannot bring about life."

Huangdi said: "What happens when the great qi enters the viscera?" Qibo said: "If disease first arises in the heart, in one day it moves to the lung, in three days it moves to the liver, in five days it moves to the spleen; if after three more days it is not resolved, the patient dies. In winter, death occurs at midnight; in summer, at midday."

If disease first arises in the lung, in three days it moves to the liver, in one day it moves to the spleen, in five days it moves to the stomach; if after ten more days it is not resolved, the patient dies. In winter, death occurs at sunset; in summer, at sunrise.

If disease first arises in the liver, in three days it moves to the spleen, in five days it moves to the stomach, in three days it moves to the kidney; if after three more days it is not resolved, the patient dies. In winter, death occurs at sunset; in summer, at early mealtime.

If disease first arises in the spleen, in one day it moves to the stomach, in two days it moves to the kidney, in three days it moves to the spinal muscles and bladder; if after ten more days it is not resolved, the patient dies. In winter, death occurs when people have settled to rest; in summer, at late mealtime.

If disease first arises in the stomach, in five days it moves to the kidney, in three days it moves to the spinal muscles and bladder, in five days it rises to the heart; if after two more days it is not resolved, the patient dies. In winter, death occurs at midnight; in summer, in the early afternoon.

If disease first arises in the kidney, in three days it moves to the spinal muscles and bladder, in three days it rises to the heart, in three days it moves to the small intestine; if after three more days it is not resolved, the patient dies. In winter, death occurs in the early morning; in summer, in the late afternoon.

If disease first arises in the bladder, in five days it moves to the kidney, in one day it moves to the small intestine, in one day it moves to the heart; if after two more days it is not resolved, the patient dies. In winter, death occurs at the cock's crow; in summer, in the late afternoon.

When diseases are transmitted in this sequence, one after another, in every such case there is a fixed time of death, and needling cannot be applied. But when the disease skips over one viscus, or two, three, or four viscera in its transmission, then it can be treated with needling.

title_en: Invasive Evil Qi Giving Rise to Dreams
paragraphs_en: Huangdi said: "I wish to hear how invasive evil qi spreads and overflows." Qibo said: "When upright-seeming evil qi invades from without to within, without yet having a fixed lodging place, it instead spreads through the viscera, unable to settle in a fixed location; it travels together with the nutritive and defensive qi, and flies about together with the hun and po souls, causing a person to be unable to rest peacefully in sleep and to dream frequently. When qi invades the bowels, there is a surplus in the exterior and a deficiency in the interior; when qi invades the viscera, there is a surplus in the interior and a deficiency in the exterior."

Huangdi said: "Do surplus and deficiency manifest in particular forms in dreams?" Qibo said: "When yin qi is exuberant, one dreams of wading through great waters and feels fear. When yang qi is exuberant, one dreams of great fires burning. When yin and yang are both exuberant, one dreams of mutual slaughter. When qi is exuberant in the upper body, one dreams of flying; when exuberant in the lower body, one dreams of falling. When extremely hungry, one dreams of taking things; when extremely full, one dreams of giving things away. When liver qi is exuberant, one dreams of anger. When lung qi is exuberant, one dreams of fear, weeping, and flying about. When heart qi is exuberant, one dreams of laughing heartily, or of fear and dread. When spleen qi is exuberant, one dreams of singing and music, and of the body feeling heavy and unable to move. When kidney qi is exuberant, one dreams of the lower back and spine coming apart and no longer connected. In all these twelve conditions of exuberance, one should drain the affected channel as soon as the qi arrives there, and the condition will be resolved at once."

When reversal qi lodges in the heart, one dreams of hills and mountains, smoke and fire. When it lodges in the lung, one dreams of flying about, and of seeing strange objects of metal and iron. When it lodges in the liver, one dreams of mountain forests and trees. When it lodges in the spleen, one dreams of hills and ridges, great marshes, and of ruined houses in wind and rain. When it lodges in the kidney, one dreams of standing at the edge of a deep abyss, or of being submerged and dwelling underwater. When it lodges in the bladder, one dreams of wandering about. When it lodges in the stomach, one dreams of food and drink. When it lodges in the large intestine, one dreams of open fields. When it lodges in the small intestine, one dreams of crowded settlements and busy crossroads. When it lodges in the gallbladder, one dreams of fighting, litigation, and self-disembowelment. When it lodges in the genitals, one dreams of sexual intercourse. When it lodges in the neck, one dreams of being beheaded. When it lodges in the shins, one dreams of walking yet being unable to go forward, and of dwelling in deep pits or in walled gardens underground. When it lodges in the thighs and arms, one dreams of ceremonial bowing and rising. When it lodges in the bladder and intestines, one dreams of urinating and defecating. In all these fifteen conditions of deficiency, one should supplement the affected channel as soon as the qi arrives there, and the condition will be resolved at once.

title_en: According with Qi: The Day Divided into Four Seasons
paragraphs_en: Huangdi said: "Now, the hundred diseases, in their origin, always arise from dryness, warmth, cold, summer-heat, wind, and rain, from yin and yang, from joy and anger, from food and drink, and from one's dwelling and manner of living; when qi combines with these factors a form results, and once it settles into a particular viscus it acquires a name — this much I understand. But why is it that in most of the hundred diseases, the patient is clear-headed at dawn, calm during the day, worsens toward evening, and is most severe at night?" Qibo said: "This is brought about by the qi of the four seasons."

Huangdi said: "I wish to hear about the qi of the four seasons." Qibo said: "Spring brings forth life, summer brings growth, autumn brings gathering-in, and winter brings storage — this is the constant pattern of qi, and human beings correspond to it as well. If one divides a single day into the four seasons, dawn corresponds to spring, midday corresponds to summer, sunset corresponds to autumn, and midnight corresponds to winter. At dawn, human qi begins to be generated and the pathogenic qi of disease declines, and so the patient is clear-headed. At midday, human qi grows to its fullness, and in its fullness it overcomes the evil qi, and so the patient is calm. Toward evening, human qi begins to decline and evil qi begins to be generated, and so the illness worsens. At midnight, human qi withdraws into the viscera, and evil qi alone occupies the body, and so the illness is most severe."

Huangdi said: "Yet there are times when this pattern is reversed — why is this?" Qibo said: "This occurs when the disease does not correspond to the qi of the four seasons, but rather when a single viscus alone governs the disease; in such cases, the illness will necessarily worsen at the time when the viscus's own qi cannot overcome the prevailing seasonal qi, and will improve at the time when its qi does overcome it." Huangdi said: "How does one treat this?" Qibo said: "By following the timing of Heaven, one can anticipate the course of the disease. One who follows this principle is a skilled physician; one who goes against it is a crude one."

Huangdi said: "Excellent. I have heard that in needling there are five transformations, which govern the five transport points. I wish to hear their measure." Qibo said: "A person has five viscera, and the five viscera have five transformations. The five transformations correspond to five transport points, and so five times five gives twenty-five transport points, corresponding to the five seasonal periods."

Huangdi said: "I wish to hear of the five transformations." Qibo said: "The liver is a male viscus; its color is green-blue, its season is spring, its note is jue, its flavor is sour, and its days are Jia and Yi. The heart is a male viscus; its color is red, its season is summer, its days are Bing and Ding, its note is zhi, and its flavor is bitter. The spleen is a female viscus; its color is yellow, its season is late summer, its days are Wu and Ji, its note is gong, and its flavor is sweet. The lung is a female viscus; its color is white, its note is shang, its season is autumn, its days are Geng and Xin, and its flavor is pungent. The kidney is a female viscus; its color is black, its season is winter, its days are Ren and Gui, its note is yu, and its flavor is salty. These are the five transformations."

Huangdi said: "How do these govern the five transport points?" Qibo said: "The viscus governs winter, and in winter one needles the Well points. The color governs spring, and in spring one needles the Spring points. The season governs summer, and in summer one needles the Stream points. The note governs late summer, and in late summer one needles the River points. The flavor governs autumn, and in autumn one needles the Sea points. This is what is meant by the five transformations governing the five transport points."

Huangdi said: "But what of the Source points — how do they fit harmoniously among these, in relation to the five transport points?" Qibo said: "The Source points alone do not correspond to the five seasonal periods; they are joined together with the River points to correspond to their proper number, and so there are six times six, thirty-six transport points in total."

Huangdi said: "What is meant by saying that the viscus governs winter, the season governs summer, the note governs late summer, the flavor governs autumn, and the color governs spring? I wish to hear the reason for this." Qibo said: "When disease lies in the viscus itself, one selects the Well point. When disease manifests as a change in complexion, one selects the Spring point. When disease alternates between mild and severe at different times, one selects the Stream point. When disease manifests as a change in the voice, one selects the River point. When the channel is full and there is stagnant blood, the disease lies in the stomach; and when disease arises from irregular eating and drinking, one selects the Sea point — hence it is said that the flavor governs the Sea point. This is what is meant by the five transformations."

title_en: Discourse on Inferring the Interior from the Exterior
paragraphs_en: Huangdi said: "I have heard the nine chapters on the nine needles; I have personally received their teaching and have grasped much of their meaning. Now, the nine needles begin with one and end with nine, yet I have not yet grasped their essential principle. The nine needles, in their smallness, admit of nothing smaller within; in their greatness, admit of nothing greater without; in their depth, cannot be surpassed from below; in their height, cannot be covered from above. They are vague and indistinct, without any fixed aperture, flowing and overflowing without limit. I know that they accord with the Way of Heaven, the affairs of man, and the transformations of the four seasons. Yet I wish to blend them together down to the finest hair, bundling them all into a single unity — can this be done?" Qibo said: "How penetrating a question this is! It is not the Way of the needle alone that is like this — the governance of a state is likewise so."

Huangdi said: "I wish to hear of the Way of the needle, not of affairs of state." Qibo said: "Now, the governance of a state depends solely upon the Way; without the Way, how could small and great, deep and shallow, all be blended together into a single unity?"

Huangdi said: "I wish to hear this through to the end." Qibo said: "Consider the sun and the moon, water and the mirror, the drum and its echo. The brightness of the sun and moon never fails to cast its reflection; the clarity of water and mirror never fails to capture form; the drum's echo in response never lags behind its sound. When something stirs and moves, the response follows in accord, and the true nature of the thing is thereby fully grasped."

Huangdi said: "How profound this is! A brightness so luminous cannot be obscured; and because it cannot be obscured, it never departs from yin and yang. By combining observation with palpation and verification, one perceives and grasps the truth, just as clear water and a bright mirror never fail to capture form. When the five notes are not manifest and the five colors are not clear, the five viscera are thrown into turbulence — when this is so, the internal and external correspond to one another just as the drum answers the drumstick, as the echo answers the sound, as the shadow resembles the form. Thus, for what is distant, one observes the exterior in order to infer the interior; for what is near, one observes the interior in order to infer the exterior. This is called the utmost expression of yin and yang, the canopy of Heaven and Earth. I ask that this be stored away in the Chamber of the Numinous Orchid, and that it not be allowed to leak out."
